iti91:1.1: This was said by the Blessed One, said by the Arahant, so I have heard:
iti91:2.1: “Monks, this is a lowly means of livelihood, alms gathering.
iti91:2.2:
It’s a form of abuse in the world [to say],
‘You go around as an alms gatherer with a bowl in your hand!’
iti91:2.3: Yet reasonable young men of good families have taken it up for a compelling reason.
iti91:2.4:
They have not been forced into it by kings or robbers, nor through debt,
through fear, nor through the loss of their livelihood, but through the thought:
iti91:2.5:
‘We are beset by birth, aging, & death, by sorrows, lamentations,
pains, distresses, & despairs, beset by stress, overcome with stress.
iti91:2.6: O, that the end of this entire mass of suffering & stress might be known!’
iti91:2.7: But this young man of good family, having gone forth in this way,
iti91:2.8:
may be greedy for sensual pleasures, strong in his passions, malevolent in mind, corrupt in his resolves,
his mindfulness muddled, unalert, uncentered, his mind scattered, & his faculties uncontrolled.
iti91:2.9:
Just as a firebrand from a funeral pyre–burning at both ends,
covered with excrement in the middle–is used as fuel neither in a village nor in the wilderness:
iti91:2.10:
I tell you that this is a simile for this person.
He has missed out on the householder’s enjoyments and does not fulfill the purpose of the contemplative life.”
iti91:3.1: He’s missed out on the householder’s enjoyment
iti91:3.2: & the purpose of the contemplative life –unfortunate man!
iti91:3.3: Ruining it, he throws it away,
iti91:3.4: perishes like a firebrand used at a funeral.
iti91:5.1: Better to eat an iron ball
iti91:5.4: unrestrained, he should eat the alms of the country.
